finding: An individual trait (conscientiousness) positively predicts successful outcomes, while another trait (neuroticism) negatively predicts extrinsic outcomes; general cognitive ability also predicts extrinsic success. Both childhood and adulthood trait measures contribute independently to predicting life outcomes.

Two individual traits (extraversion and agreeableness) positively predict a behavioral outcome; the effect of a third trait (openness) disappears when controlling for other traits. The behavioral outcome predicts multiple effectiveness measures.

Childhood traits predict adult emotional characteristics; emotional negativity is broadly linked to maladaptive outcomes across both childhood and adulthood; low behavioral constraint predicts antisocial conduct longitudinally.

A dispositional trait is conceptualized and operationalized with strong measurement properties, predicts a specific achievement outcome, and when measured as a state predicts daily experiences; the trait has incremental validity beyond existing constructs.

Individuals attributed higher levels on a positive social attribute are perceived as lower on undesirable personality dimensions and higher on desirable ones; these perception patterns are consistent across different observer assessment methods.

The effect of a decision type (action vs. inaction) on emotional regret reverses depending on prior outcomes; negative prior outcomes flip the typical pattern, making inaction produce more regret than action.
Mere association with an item increases preference for that item across multiple languages; the effect manifests even when comparing identical stimuli and holds cross-culturally.
People systematically attend to nominal rather than real values in economic judgments; this bias arises from a dual representation process where nominal and real evaluations interact.
Knowing an outcome increases perceived predictability of that outcome retrospectively; forcing consideration of alternative outcomes reduces this hindsight bias.
A metacognitive belief (about optimal test strategy) persists despite contradictory empirical evidence; differential memorability of outcomes explains why people maintain this false belief.

People attribute greater unpredictability, multiple possible futures, and stronger causal control to their own behavior compared to peers' behavior; this bias is consistent across multiple dimensions of agency.
Individuals perceive cognitive and motivational biases in others more than in themselves; even explicit awareness of how a bias could apply to oneself does not reduce this asymmetry.
When testing hypotheses, people maximize the evidential value of confirming outcomes rather than their probability; motivational context enhances this tendency.

Inaction is judged as less immoral than action producing identical outcomes; this judgment arises from attributions that inaction does not cause outcomes, and generalizes a useful heuristic beyond its justified domain.

People estimate their own chances of experiencing positive outcomes as above average and negative outcomes as below average relative to peers; this optimistic bias varies with outcome desirability, perceived controllability, and other factors.

Preferences for an attribute vary between joint and single evaluation contexts; when people in joint evaluation predict or choose for single evaluation experiences, they overpredict the difference an attribute will make to satisfaction.
A default option substantially influences choice behavior; simply changing the default can produce large differences in aggregate outcomes.
Adjustments from a starting value terminate prematurely; insufficient adjustment occurs unless people actively search for a more accurate estimate.
An event occurring in the future is valued more than an identical event in the past; this temporal asymmetry is absent within individuals and is mediated by differential affect.
People systematically predict others to be more risk-seeking than themselves; this self-other discrepancy disappears when targets of prediction are specific rather than abstract.
